content:
Hi guys,

I wonder what is the status of writing C APIs for Rust code in the kernel?

I know cbindgen is not going to work since it requires cargo, but I wonder if the usual approach of declaring functions with C ABI (pub extern "C" fn foo()) coupled with a compatible C header file will work?

My intention here is to rewrite a small kernel library in Rust and then provide a C API for existing users.
We don't have cbindgen, but you can still do it manually. Rust Binder does that to let binderfs call into Rust Binder.

Yeah, we don't have cbindgen because we do not really need it yet, but we may want to explore it at some point. For the moment, as Alice says, you can write your own header.

However:

I know cbindgen is not going to work since it requires cargo

What do you mean? It does not require Cargo to use it, i.e. they offer a CLI tool like bindgen does.

Perhaps you mean that they offer the option to fetch the metadata of a crate from Cargo (because it is the only example they give in their front page), but you can also pass it your own crate root, e.g. cbindgen --lang c -- x.rs.

Hi Miguel, Alice, long time no see!

Miguel, I was going to write on how I was sure what you said did not work, then I tried it...and it worked :man_facepalming:

I have been working on some Rust code in Mesa and did not know this was possible.

Why don't they have this example up top on their readme is beyond me, I have been laboriously writing some proc_macros to check my own hand-written header for ABI compatibility and you've just saved me a lot of work.

Well, in my defense their go-to example complains because there's no cargo, and I looked at the source code and it just internalized that it was in fact needed.

Alice, thanks for pointing me to binder as a reference, that's extra helpful.

My pleasure -- I am glad it helped!

Yeah, I don't know why they don't mention that (I thought the reason was that it was meant to be in the docs.md, but it does not seem mentioned there either, though the build.rs approach is documented there).

It happens also in other Rust projects, e.g. the Clippy README.md a couple years ago did not mention how to use it without Cargo, so sent a PR to fix that. Perhaps they may be happy to add it (@Emilio Cobos Álvarez), e.g. something like:

diff --git a/README.md b/README.md
index 22183e4b3377..3262ece19084 100644
--- a/README.md
+++ b/README.md
@@ -66,6 +66,12 @@ Then all you need to do is run it:
 cbindgen --config cbindgen.toml --crate my_rust_library --output my_header.h
 ```

+or, if your project does not use Cargo, you can pass it the crate root source file:
+
+```text
+cbindgen -config cbindgen.toml --output my_header.h my_crate.rs
+```
+
 This produces a header file for C++.  For C, add the `--lang c` switch.

 See `cbindgen --help` for more options.

We have used cbingen to generate the header files for the Rust crate by command line. One problem is that cbindgen can not handle structures like Arc and Spinlock. The C side only has a typedef which can only be used as a pointer. Thus we have to manually write a structure in the C side that has the same size and alignment as the Rust.
75924d8a66b26839c0957562a370c93.png
